informal: Suppose $T \in \mathcal{L}(V)$. Prove that if $U_{1}, \ldots, U_{m}$ are subspaces of $V$ invariant under $T$, then $U_{1}+\cdots+U_{m}$ is invariant under $T$.
lean:
theorem Axler_exercise_5_1 {F V : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Field F]
  [Module F V] {L : V →ₗ[F] V} {n : ℕ} (U : Fin n → Submodule F V)
  (hU : ∀ i : Fin n, Submodule.map L (U i) = U i) :
  Submodule.map L (∑ i : Fin n, U i : Submodule F V) =
  (∑ i : Fin n, U i : Submodule F V)
reasoning: The informal statement says each U_i is T-invariant, which standardly means T(U_i) ⊆ U_i, and the conclusion is T(U_1+⋯+U_m) ⊆ U_1+⋯+U_m. The Lean signature instead encodes both the hypothesis and the conclusion using `Submodule.map L (U i) = U i`, i.e. T(U_i) = U_i (set-theoretic equality, equivalent to T being surjective onto each U_i when restricted). This is a strictly stronger notion than invariance in both places. So the Lean theorem has a stronger hypothesis (does not follow from the informal claim) and a stronger conclusion (the informal proof would not establish equality). The two theorems are not logically comparable: the informal one is about ⊆-invariance while the Lean one is about exact preservation, so neither implies the other.
